title: 📊 Schedule C Deductions: Full IRS Approved List 2026
description:
The complete list of Schedule C deductible business expenses approved by the IRS in 2026, with practical examples. Plus the UK self employed allowable expenses guide.

Personal finance, Schedule C deductions, freelancer write offs, allowable expenses UK 2026.

segments.voice: Schedule C is where US freelancers report business income and expenses. The IRS published the complete list of deductible business expense categories in Publication 535 and the Schedule C instructions. Knowing what is on that list, and what is not, is the difference between paying full freight and paying the right amount. Today, the full breakdown.

The fundamental test, ordinary and necessary. Per IRC section 162 and IRS Publication 535, every deductible expense must be both ordinary, common in your trade, and necessary, helpful and appropriate for your business. Lavish or extravagant expenses can be challenged. The standard is the same for a software developer in Seattle and a farmer in Kansas, just applied differently.

Line eight, advertising. All advertising expenses are fully deductible. Google Ads, Facebook Ads, Yelp ads, sponsored content, business cards, billboards, branded merchandise, conference booth rentals, all qualify. The IRS does not cap advertising expenses but they must be genuinely promotional, not personal entertainment in disguise.

Line nine, car and truck expenses. Either the standard mileage rate at sixty-seven cents per business mile for two thousand twenty-six, or actual expenses with depreciation. Tolls and parking are separate. Personal use must be allocated out. We covered this in detail in the vehicle deduction video.

Line ten, commissions and fees. Payments to other contractors, sales commissions, finder's fees, referral fees, all deductible here. If you pay any single contractor over six hundred dollars in a year, you must issue Form 1099-NEC by January thirty-first per IRC section 6041A. Failing to issue 1099s can result in penalty under IRC section 6721.

Line eleven, contract labor. Same idea as line ten but specifically for direct labor on services rather than commissions. The 1099-NEC threshold and rules apply identically. Many freelancers split this into commissions versus contract labor depending on the nature of the relationship, but they end up in the same Schedule C line in any case.

Line twelve, depletion. Specific to natural resource industries like oil, gas, mining, and timber. Most freelancers and service businesses skip this line entirely. If you do operate in a depletable industry, IRS Publication 535 chapter nine has the detailed rules.

Line thirteen, depreciation. Equipment, furniture, vehicles, computers, and other long-lived assets are deducted over their useful life via MACRS depreciation per IRS Publication 946. Section 179 lets you immediately expense most equipment up to one million two hundred thousand for two thousand twenty-six. Bonus depreciation is sixty percent for assets placed in service in two thousand twenty-six.

Line fourteen, employee benefits. If you have employees other than yourself, you can deduct premiums for health, dental, and vision insurance, life insurance, retirement plan contributions for employees, employee assistance programs, and education assistance under IRC section 127. The deduction does not cover benefits paid to yourself as a sole proprietor.

Line fifteen, insurance other than health. Liability insurance, errors and omissions, cyber liability, business property insurance, and business interruption insurance are all deductible here. Workers compensation insurance for employees also goes here. Do not put your personal auto insurance, homeowners, or life insurance here, those have separate rules.

Line sixteen, interest on business loans. Mortgage interest on business property goes on line sixteen-a. Other business interest goes on line sixteen-b. Personal interest is generally not deductible per the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, with limited exceptions for mortgage and investment interest tracked separately on Schedule A.

Line seventeen, legal and professional services. CPA fees, attorney fees for business matters, business consultant fees, bookkeeping services, all deductible. The fee for the CPA who prepared your tax return covering Schedule C is deductible. The portion for personal tax matters is not. Most CPAs split the bill between business and personal portions on the invoice.

Line eighteen, office expense. Office supplies, postage, printing, software subscriptions used for business administration. Pens, paper, ink cartridges, copy paper, file folders, the everyday materials that keep an office running. Software like QuickBooks, Notion, Slack, Zoom, all qualify here for service freelancers.

Line nineteen, pension and profit-sharing plans. If you have employees and offer a 401(k), SEP-IRA, or SIMPLE-IRA, the employer contributions to their accounts go here. Your own contributions as the owner go on Schedule 1, not Schedule C. Many freelancers get this wrong and either double-deduct or miss the deduction entirely.

Line twenty, rent or lease. Rent on a separate office space, equipment leases, vehicle leases for business vehicles, all go here. The home office deduction has its own line, line thirty, not line twenty. If you sublease office space or share a coworking arrangement, that rent is deductible on line twenty.

Line twenty-one, repairs and maintenance. Routine maintenance and repairs that keep equipment functioning are deductible. Improvements that extend useful life or add capacity must be capitalized and depreciated, per IRC section 263 and the tangible property regulations. The line between repair and improvement is one of the most audited issues in small business taxes.

Line twenty-two, supplies. Materials consumed in the course of doing business. A graphic designer's printing supplies, a baker's flour and sugar, a dentist's gloves and disposable supplies. Different from equipment because supplies are consumable, used up in the normal course of business, while equipment lasts and gets depreciated.

Line twenty-three, taxes and licenses. State and local business taxes, business license fees, professional license renewals, sales tax paid on business property purchases. Federal income tax and self-employment tax are not deductible. State and local income taxes go on Schedule A as itemized deductions, not Schedule C.

Line twenty-four, travel and meals. Travel expenses for business trips are fully deductible, transportation, lodging, fifty percent of meals, tips, internet, dry cleaning while away. Meals are limited to fifty percent under IRC section 274(n). You must be away from your tax home overnight for travel rules to apply.

Line twenty-five, utilities. Electricity, gas, water, internet, phone for business locations. The home office deduction handles utility allocation for the home office portion separately on line thirty. Direct utilities for a separate office or commercial space go on line twenty-five.

Line twenty-six, wages. Wages paid to actual employees go here. Owner draws and distributions do not. If you operate as an S corporation, your own W-2 salary from the corporation is wages on line twenty-six. Sole proprietors do not pay themselves wages, they take draws which are not deductible.

Line twenty-seven-a, other expenses. The catch-all category. List specific items separately on line forty-eight on the Schedule C continuation. Common entries include subscriptions to industry publications, professional dues, education and training, bank fees, merchant processing fees, marketing platforms not classified as advertising. The IRS audits this line carefully because it tends to attract questionable items.

Line thirty, home office expense. The home office deduction has its own dedicated line per IRC section 280A. The simplified method is five dollars per square foot up to fifteen hundred dollars. The actual expense method requires Form 8829 with detailed allocations of utilities, insurance, depreciation, repairs.

What is not deductible under any line. Personal expenses, fines and penalties for breaking the law, lobbying expenses with limited exceptions, club dues for entertainment clubs, political contributions, life insurance premiums on business owners except in specific entity arrangements. The IRS lists each of these explicitly in Publication 535 chapter eleven.
